issue_title: Renewal fails without the secret argument
issue_body:
I've got a crontab entry to renew the certs periodically but when it ran this month I got the cryptic error
```
 * Stopping web server apache2
 * 
Updating letsencrypt and virtual environment dependencies.......
Running with virtualenv: sudo /home/ubuntu/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/letsencrypt certonly --standalone --email spiro.agnew@whitehouse.gov --agree-tos -d trix.dci.pertino.com
User chose to cancel the operation and may reinvoke the client.
 * Starting web server apache2
 * 
```
When after looking through the code found you needed a secret, undocumented option
```
--renew-by-default
```
on your "certonly" request to get it to actually work as you'd expect.

issue_title: Provide feedback when authorizing large lists of domains
issue_body: We have a report from someone issuing a cert with 90 names: https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/solved-rate-limit-with-san-certificate-90-hostnames/4010/7. They said the screen seemed to hang while it was working on the challenges, so they killed it. If we don't already have some sort of "in progress" indicator we should add on.

issue_title: --install should default to installing whatever certs you got with "letsencrypt certonly"
issue_body: and only demand `--cert-path` and `--key-path` if none of those make sense.

issue_title: consume(timeout=1) takes 5 seconds
issue_body:
Description
===========
`consume(num_messages=100, timeout=1)` takes about 5 seconds.


How to reproduce
================

consumer.py
```py
import time
from confluent_kafka import Consumer

consumer = Consumer({
    'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost',
    'group.id': 'example_consumer',
    'default.topic.config': {
        'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest'
     }
})

consumer.subscribe(['test_q'])

while True:
    while True:
        print('.')
        batch = consumer.consume(num_messages=100, timeout=1)
        if len(batch)>0:
            break
    print(len(batch))
    print('---')
    time.sleep(1)
```

producer.py
```py
import time
from confluent_kafka import Producer

producer = Producer({
    'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost',
    'queue.buffering.max.messages': 10000000,  # 1e7
})

i = 0

while True:
    i += 1
    print(i)

    msg = 'msg ' + str(i)
    producer.poll(0)
    producer.produce('test_q', msg.encode(), callback=None)
    time.sleep(0.1)
```

open 3 terminals and run

1. `python consumer.py`
2. `python producer.py`
3. `./bin/kafka-console-consumer.sh --zookeeper localhost --topic test_q`

You can see the immediate consumption in the terminal3.
The producer sends 10 messages/sec so I expect the consumer to return the batch of size 10.

However, terminal1 prints every 5 seconds. consumer.consume doesn't return for 5 seconds.



Checklist
=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [x] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`): ('0.11.4', 721920), ('0.11.4', 722175)
 - [x] Apache Kafka broker version: 2.12-1.1.0
 - [ ] Client configuration: `{...}`
 - [ ] Operating system:
 - [ ] Provide client logs (with `'debug': '..'` as necessary)
 - [ ] Provide broker log excerpts
 - [ ] Critical issue

issue_title: Producer: different callback per message
issue_body:
Version: librdkafka 0.9.1 and last pip confluent lib

```python

from confluent_kafka import Producer

p = Producer({'bootstrap.servers':'kafkahost'})

def callback(err,msg,k):
  print "callback received k: %s and v: %s" % (k,msg.value())

data = {
  'k1':'v1',
  'k2':'v2',
  'k3':'v3',
}

for k,v in data.iteritems():
  print "sending k: %s" % k
  p.produce('patricktest', v, callback=lambda err,msg: callback(err,msg,k))

p.flush()
```

The result is:
```
Mac:~$ python producertest.py
sending k: k3
sending k: k2
sending k: k1
callback received k: k1 and v: v3
callback received k: k1 and v: v2
callback received k: k1 and v: v1
```

and I would expect in the three last lines to be k3 k2 k1 rather than three times k1

After looking at the source code, it seems that each time a callback is defined, it's defined as a global parameter for the whole producer, rather than per message.

This breaks the general expectation that if you give a callback to a function/method, it's THIS function which will be called. I didn't find a workaround either to give arbitrary extra data to the callback - which was what I attempted via this intermediate lambda.

--Thanks

issue_title: Converting an rrule to a string and back results in loss of timezone information in the _dtstart attribute
issue_body:
In the following script:

```python
from dateutil.rrule import rrule, rrulestr, DAILY
from dateutil.parser import parse

start = parse("8 Feb 2017 14:00 UTC")
my_rrule = rrule(DAILY, dtstart=start)

my_rrule2 = rrulestr(str(my_rrule))

assert my_rrule._dtstart == my_rrule2._dtstart
```

the assertion leads to a

```python
TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes
```
This is because `my_rrule._dtstart` has the representation

```python
datetime.datetime(2017, 2, 8, 14, 0, tzinfo=tzutc())
```
whereas the 'reconstructed' `my_rrule2._dtstart` is

```python
datetime.datetime(2017, 2, 8, 14, 0)
```
In other words, converting an `rrule` to a string and back (using `__str__` and `rrulestr`, respectively) leads to a loss of time zone information of the `_dtstart` attribute. This would seem undesirable, since the original and reconstructed object are no longer equivalent.

issue_title: Should not be allowed to resolve file: URIs from http/http
issue_body:
Resolving a `file` URI from a `http`/`https` base URI is a security concern (and reproducibility concern!)

```python
fetcher.urljoin("http://example.com/bar/soup.cwl", "file:///C:/Users/fred/foo.cwl")
```

I think the above should always throw `ValueError` if the base URI is not in scheme `file:`.

In addition, considering #129 / #110 on Windows resolving `C:/something` should also fail if the base is not a `file:`

issue_title: Certifi has problem to verify SSL
issue_body:
I found that pyelasticsearch depends on certifi. It'll break `requests.get` with some HTTPS site. Here's the issue on certifi https://github.com/certifi/python-certifi/issues/26

The current workaround if I want to verify is I need to specify CA bundle file everywhere I use `requests.get`.
```
requests.get('https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/..', verify='/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt')
```
or
```
requests.get('https://s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/..', verify=certifi.old_where())
```

issue_title: Duplication issue with query using filter on M2M relation in Meta class for Resource.
issue_body:
Hi all, I've encountered an odd issue that results in the output from a GET call containing duplicate objects due to the manner in which the Django query is constructed and yields rows.

For off, I'm using Django 1.7.9 with Tastypie 0.12.1. Database is Posgresql 9.4

The setup required to duplicate this issue is a little involved.

First off, the models:
```
class A(models.Model):
    pass

class B(models.Model):
    instances_of_a = models.ManyToManyField(A, related_name='instances_of_b', null=True, blank=True)
```

now for the problematic Resource definition:
```
class AResource(ModelResource):
    class Meta:
        queryset = A.objects.all()

class BResource(ModelResource):
    instances_of_a = fields.ManyToManyField(AResource, 'instances_of_a', full=True, null=True)

    class Meta:
        queryset = B.objects.filter(instances_of_a__isnull=False)
```

When a GET is performed against BResource, any instances of B that are associated with more than one A object via the instances_of_a M2M relation will be returned multiple times in the response from BResource. The number of times an instance of B will be returned is equal to the number of A objects associated with it by the instances_of_a M2M relation.

The reason for this is due to the SQL Django generates to produce the Queryset. The SQL in question looks like this
```
SELECT
"database_schema_name"."id"
FROM "B"
 INNER JOIN "A"
   ON ("B"."id" =
       "B_A_Assn"."B_id")
WHERE ("B_A_Assn"."A_id" IS NOT NULL);
```

The result of this SQL is the issue describe above wherein there will be duplicate items in the queryset.

Probably the simplest solution to this is to not use a related filter in the fashion described but I would image it would be possible to detect for this kind of condition and handle it?

issue_title: Support pathlib.Path objects for paths
issue_body:
The [pathlib](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html) module was added to the standard library in Python 3.4. The expected behavior is to be able to path either a string or a `pathlib.Path` object when referencing a file or directory. For example, this behavior is demonstrated in `open`, `gzip.open`, and `os.walk`.

**Example expected behavior in mutagen:**
```
import pathlib
from mutagen.id3 import ID3

path = pathlib.Path('somefile.mp3')
audio = ID3(path)
```

**Workaround:**
Casting the ``pathlib.Path``object as as a string.
```
import pathlib
from mutagen.id3 import ID3

path = pathlib.Path('somefile.mp3')
audio = ID3(str(path))
```

issue_title: Inconsistency when mixing integers and bitvectors
issue_body:
When mixing integers and bitvectors, an inconsistency can occur. See

```smt2
(define-fun UInt32ToInt ((x Int)) Int (ite (>= x 2147483648) (- x 4294967296) x))
(define-fun ToInt32 ((x Int)) (_ BitVec 32) ((_ int2bv 32) x))

(define-fun f ((a Int)) Int (UInt32ToInt (bv2int (bvor (ToInt32 (- 50)) (ToInt32 a)))))

(simplify (f 3))

(declare-const x Int)
(assert (= (- 49) (f x)))
(check-sat)
```
produces
```smt2
(- 49)
unsat
```

which is clearly contradictory. This can be run here: http://rise4fun.com/Z3/V4eI If you rearrange it so `f` just returns a bitvector rather than an integer (and we search for the bitvector equivalent to -49), we get `sat`, however. This suggests the problem is with `bv2int`.

issue_title: Typos in the documentation
issue_body:
Hi everyone,

i want to warn about some possible typos:

1) In the documentation of orthogonal initialization there is this equation: sqrt(2/(1+alpha**2)

, shouldn't it be: sqrt(2/(1+alpha**2))

2) In the documentation of Spatial Transform Layer:

issue_title: CFGAccurate parameter "starts" making making analysis more wrong
issue_body:
Right now CFGAccurate/CFGFast create incomplete CFGs and miss a lot of functions. 
There is no analysis of the binary to spot functions and executable code. 
Instead there is only recursive descent. 
That means the first mistake angr makes (e.g. "angr.state_plugins.callstack | Returning to an unexpected address") makes the whole tree behind that mistake inaccessible to analysis.
(Are you interested in these mistakes? Should I open issues every time a binary has incomplete CFG?)

Anyway.
If I understand @fish correctly there is the "starts" parameter as a workaround.
I can supply additional starting points to CFGAccurate.

But "starts" breaks analysis that previously worked.

Example: 
If I don't use "starts", the binary is parsed correctly.
E.g. the following function:

_Function sub_11320 [0x11320]
  Has return: True
  Returning: True
  Blocks: [0x11320, 0x11389, 0x1136b, 0x1138c, 0x11330, 0x11377]

But when I supply all function addresses in "starts", the function gets analyzed incorrectly:

  Returning: False
  Blocks: [0x11320, 0x11389, 0x1136b, 0x1138c, 0x11330, 0x11377]

issue_title: easy way to format hello messages?
issue_body:
I'm attempting to work with some Brocade IronWare devices. They don't seem to like this as a HELLO message:

```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
    <nc:hello xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0">
        <nc:capabilities>
            <nc:capability>urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0</nc:capability>
            <nc:capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:base:1.0</nc:capability>
        </nc:capabilities>
    </nc:hello>]]>]]>
```

They do however appear to like this:

```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
    <hello xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0">
        <capabilities>
            <capability>urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0</capability>
            <capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:base:1.0</capability>
        </capabilities>
    </hello>]]>]]>
```

Is there a way to customize the HELLO message format? I worked around this a few years ago by overriding `HelloHandler`'s `build()` method in `transport/session.py` - these hacks still work but are kind of ugly, and they're also causing other methods I want to use ie `get`, `get_config` etc to fail (I'm doing something wrong with the XML handling, I can smell it).

Also is there an easy way to alter the capabilities that ncclient provides to a server before any connection is initiated? I remember you used to be able to assign a list of capabilities to `manager.CAPABILITIES` a few years ago but this appears to no longer be the case. My Brocades also don't appear to like the long capability list that ncclient provides by default and I would like to define my own.

Thanks.

issue_title: Excessive Delay when polling vehicle.attitude, etc.
issue_body:
I am working on a multi-agent fixed wing UAV formation project. The agents share information over UDP broadcast via a raspberry pi v3B. In the main control thread, I poll various attributes (attitude, position, channels, etc) and am using roll angle as a test case for debugging.

I have found (by profiling) that the program spends the overwhelming majority of time waiting (to have a reasonable sampling time). This is expected. I have found that as the other threads consume resources (not many resources, but some), the reads, to, say, vehicle.attitude.roll produce values that are delayed by up to about 1.5 seconds. The resulting values are generally unique, but lag behind real-time by that amount.

I dug into the code, and it looks like a queue is being used in mavlink.py to get messages from pymavlink to dronekit. I suspect this queue is not being emptied fast enough. If this is, in fact, a likely cause, what is the cleanest way manually process all messages in this queue to get real-time data into the vehicle object?

issue_title: Check for current hostname vs job hostname Localhost
issue_body:
Just a minor thing to fix, but it would be nice if the localhost backend would realise that if it's not on the same localhost as a given job that it cannot monitor, or alter it in any way.

Imo, it would be more informative to give an error such as:
```couldn't kill job x.y, job is running on myOtherHost (PID: 1234) please check it's killed there)``` rather than the 6 or 7 errrors relating to missing files that Ganga couldn't possibly know about.

I'd say this is relatively low priority but I keep seeing this in testing when I move from machine to machine and it gives the impression the code here is a little fragile even though it's not.

issue_title: Old DiracFile not accessible
issue_body:
Due to some changes within the class initialization framework DiracFile old classes which don't have `defaultSE` will fail to load from XML.
This prevents the jobs from loading.

I think I've understood how this happens and the fix should be fairly quick and effective once I get a chance and it would be good to see in 6.3.1 so we can load old jobs properly (only in 6.3.x).

issue_title: PPO2 Normalizing advantage across multiple episodes
issue_body:
In the ```ppo2.py``` file in PPO2 implementation, we normalize a batch of advantage data on line 53. (```advs = (advs - advs.mean()) / (advs.std() + 1e-8)```

However looking at the  ```Runner``` class that collects the batch data for a fixed number of steps, it seems it's possible to obtain a batch of data collected from multiple episodes.

If this is the case, shouldn't we be normalizing the advantage across each episode in the batch, instead of over the entire batch? Wouldn't this introduce some bias to our advantage estimates?

issue_title: Can't use a Union type in a connection
issue_body:
If I have something like this:

```python
class SomeTypeOne(ObjectType):
    pass

class SomeTypeTwo(ObjectType):
    pass

class MyUnion(Union):
    class Meta:
        types = (SomeTypeOne, SomeTypeTwo)

class Query(AbstractType):
    things = relay.ConnectionField(MyUnion)
```

it fails with `AssertionError: Only ObjectTypes can have a Node interface.`  If the last line is changed to:

```python
things = graphene.Field(MyUnion)
```

then it works fine.

Is there another way to do this, or is it just not supported yet?

issue_title: How to lazy_import relay Node Connection / Edge?
issue_body:
I am trying to port the following line (in ClientIDMutation) into using `lazy_import`:

```python
edge = graphene.Field(lambda: import_module('some_package.schema.nodes').Event.Connection.Edge)
```

I have tried:
```python
edge = graphene.Field(lazy_import('some_package.schema.nodes.Event.Connection.Edge'))
# and
edge = graphene.Field(lazy_import('some_package.schema.nodes.Event'.Connection.Edge))
```

The first does not work. `lazy_import` uses `dotted_path.rsplit('.', 1)` which cannot recognise this kind of deeply nested class. It throws `ImportError: No module named Event.Connection`

The second does not work either, throwing `AttributeError: 'functools.partial' object has no attribute 'Connection'`

Is there a recommended way to write this using `lazy_import`? Or maybe I should be writing my `ClientIDMutation` in another way?

Thanks!

issue_title: Argument.__eq__ always true if passed an Argument
Provide wheel distribution on PyPI
issue_body:
Hi!

Please could this package also by made available as a wheel on PyPI?
https://pypi.python.org/pypi/graphene/1.4.1

Wheels have the following advantages:
* Faster installation time
* Allows the wheel cache to work even when using the new pip hash-checking mode
* Allows tools to statically inspect the package metadata without having to execute untrusted code.
* ...and more - see: http://pythonwheels.com

This package is pure Python and supports both Python 2 and 3, so in theory could be distributed as a "universal wheel":
https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/distributing-packages/#wheels

...however I notice that there's some `__builtin__` vs `builtins` workaround going on here:
https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/557ec44a135f46f865fe55634c9145a2e07b9ee2/setup.py#L6-L17

...which I believe means separate wheels have to be generated for each of Python 2 and 3. 

The Python packaging guide recommends using [Twine](https://github.com/pypa/twine) to upload packages:
https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/distributing-packages/#uploading-your-project-to-pypi

As such, the steps for building/uploading a new release are then:

```bash
$ pip2 install -U twine setuptools wheel
$ pip3 install -U setuptools wheel
$ rm -rf dist/ build/
$ python2 setup.py sdist bdist_wheel
# Work around https://bitbucket.org/pypa/wheel/issues/147/bdist_wheel-should-start-by-cleaning-up
$ rm -rf build/
$ python3 setup.py bdist_wheel
$ twine upload dist/*
```

The PyPI package listing will then display three entries for the new release, one with type "Source" and two of type "Python Wheel" (one for each of Python 2 and 3).

Many thanks :-)

issue_body: I'm working with the latest version of sorl-thumbnail (12.4a1) in a distributed system and the rendering is way too slow, around 1s/thumbnail. ##### Software used - sorl-thumbnail 12.4a1 - Python 3.5 - Django 1.11.3 - Google Cloud Storage - Redis 4.0 (through a Unix socket) - Ubuntu 17.04 (GNU/Linux 4.10.0-32-generic x86_64). ##### Expected behavior The thumbnail rendering should be practically instantaneous if cached. ##### Actual behavior The thumbnail rendering when cached takes around 1 second _(0.5-1.5s)_. ##### Steps to reproduce the behavior ``` {% thumbnail user.image.image as img %} {% empty %} {% endthumbnail %} ``` ##### Additional info I've identified a few problems: **1) Line 153: `output = self.nodelist_file.render(context)` takes ~1 second to render** ``` Timer unit: 1e-06 s Total time: 0.604442 s File: /Users/[...]/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sorl/thumbnail/templatetags/thumbnail.py Function: _render at line 129 Line # Hits Time Per Hit % Time Line Contents ============================================================== 129 @profile_each_line 130 def _render(self, context): 131 1 53 53.0 0.0 file_ = self.file_.resolve(context) 132 1 6 6.0 0.0 geometry = self.geometry.resolve(context) 133 1 0 0.0 0.0 options = {} 134 3 3 1.0 0.0 for key, expr in self.options: 135 2 2 1.0 0.0 noresolve = {'True': True, 'False': False, 'None': None} 136 2 13 6.5 0.0 value = noresolve.get(text_type(expr), expr.resolve(context)) 137 2 2 1.0 0.0 if key == 'options': 138 options.update(value) 139 else: 140 2 2 1.0 0.0 options[key] = value 141 142 1 1832 1832.0 0.3 thumbnail = get_thumbnail(file_, geometry, **options) 143 144 1 2 2.0 0.0 if not thumbnail or (isinstance(thumbnail, DummyImageFile) and self.nodelist_empty): 145 if self.nodelist_empty: 146 return self.nodelist_empty.render(context) 147 else: 148 return '' 149 150 1 2 2.0 0.0 if self.as_var: 151 1 9 9.0 0.0 context.push() 152 1 3 3.0 0.0 context[self.as_var] = thumbnail 153 1 602506 602506.0 99.7 output = self.nodelist_file.render(context) 154 1 6 6.0 0.0 context.pop() 155 else: 156 output = thumbnail.url 157 158 1 1 1.0 0.0 return output ``` **2) The class `ImageFile(BaseImageFile)` from sorl.thumbnail.images takes between 0.5-1 second if used with Google Cloud Storage from _django-storages_ due to the methods of `self.storage`** This lag is especially noticeable in this section of the previously mentioned class: ``` if hasattr(self.storage, 'location'): location = self.storage.location if not self.storage.location.endswith("/"): location += "/" if self.name.startswith(location): self.name = self.name[len(location):] ``` ##### HINT: I think many of the performance problems could be related to the function `url` of ImageFile(BaseImageFile) because the APIs of cloud storages can be a bit tricky. ``` Timer unit: 1e-06 s Total time: 0.832383 s File: /Users/[...]/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sorl/thumbnail/images.py Function: url at line 159 Line # Hits Time Per Hit % Time Line Contents ============================================================== 159 @property 160 @profile_each_line 161 def url(self): 162 1 832383 832383.0 100.0 return self.storage.url(self.name) ```

issue_title: Suspicious VerifyOrExit() in Local::SendServerDataNotification()
issue_body:
I'm going through the code and I've some concerns about this condition in `network_data_local.cpp:211`


    VerifyOrExit((mMle.GetDeviceMode() & Mle::ModeTlv::kModeFFD) == 0 ||
                 (mMle.IsRouterRoleEnabled() == false) ||
                 (mMle.GetDeviceState() >= Mle::kDeviceStateRouter) ||
                 (mMle.GetActiveRouterCount() >= mMle.GetRouterUpgradeThreshold()),
                 error = kThreadError_InvalidState);


If any of the conditions evaluates to true the verify is positive (will not exit). So, for example, `mMle.GetDeviceMode() & Mle::ModeTlv::kModeFFD) == 0` doesn't trigger an error. Is that the intended behavior?

In addition:
1) According to Thread 1.1 spec children can act as servers, hence I'm not clear why `mMle.GetDeviceState() >= Mle::kDeviceStateRouter` is needed.
2) What is the reason to check for active router count?

issue_title: Function taking Eigen::Ref matrix cannot be called with 1xN numpy array
issue_body:
#### Issue description

I have a C++ function taking a ``const Eigen::Ref<const Eigen::MatrixXd>&`` input parameter. It accepts matrices of arbitrary dimensions. The pybind11-generated binding works fine when I pass NxM or Nx1 matrices. For example:

```
>>> my_function(numpy.zeros((4,3)))
>>> my_function(numpy.zeros((3,1)))
```

But I get an error when I try to pass a 1xN matrix:

```
>>> my_function(numpy.zeros((1,3)))

TypeError: my_function(): incompatible function arguments. The following argument types are supported:
    1. (numpy.ndarray[float64[m, n], flags.f_contiguous]) -> arg0: None

Invoked with: array([[ 0.,  0.,  0.]])
```

Is this behaviour intended? The "Vectors versus column/row matrices" section of the pybind11 documentation only speaks about automatic conversion of Numpy arrays to Eigen matrices and row/column vectors, but it seems that the case described above is not really covered in the discussion. Please note that, in my case, I am not dealing with vectors, only 2d matrices of arbitrary dimensions. 

When I change the signature of my c++ function to taking a ``const Eigen::MatrixXd&`` instead of an ``Eigen::Ref``, then it works for any 2d Numpy array including 1xN arrays. However, I would prefer the ``Eigen::Ref`` solution, because it avoids copying of data if certain conditions are met.

#### Reproducible example code

I have attached a minimal code example that demonstrates the problem:
[matrix_issue.zip](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/files/847647/matrix_issue.zip)

issue_title: [Proposal] Generate API objects from swagger spec
issue_body:
After using this library for a while, I can see a few areas in which the flow for authoring specs could be improved. Here's a sketch of a design, to get a conversation going, and to see if you guys have done any work in this area.

The general idea I'd like to propose is to load object definitions from the Swagger spec files that the k8s API exposes, and use those object definitions to generate the set of classes that can be used to build specs.

A couple options:
1) There are dynamic libraries like https://github.com/Yelp/bravado which load a Swagger spec into memory.
2) Something like [swagger-codegen](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-codegen) could be used to statically compile a client, which would then give IDE autocompetion. (I favour this option).

Now you can write code like

```
my_pod = Pod(
  containers=[
    Container(name='first', image='alpine'),
  ],
  ..
)
```

With each of these objects having all fields defined by the swagger spec. Tab completion for your Kubernetes specs!

The other benefit of this approach is that you no longer need to add classes manually for each Kind, which implies less work to support a new major API version. There are some questions here though; would the static client definitions be checked in to the codebase (giving completions out of the box, without any additional build steps), or loaded by the library user so that they have the correct definitions for their cluster (this would be required to add dynamically added third-party-resources). 

Either way it's simple to generate the client; you can run `swagger-codegen generate -i http://kubernetes.io/kubernetes/swagger-spec/ -l python -o k8s-swagger ` to load the public swagger definition and generate a fresh client implementation. https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-codegen#homebrew has more detailed instructions if you want to play further.

I'd be interested in your thoughts on this, before I go too much further in exploring the implementation.

issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
Ubuntu 16.04.3

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
It self upgraded to 0.19.0

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
./certbout-auto

No new domains detected in list, only the old previously detected ones.

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
It does not detect new config files in apache2/sites-avaliable only the previos ones detected.

issue_title: add a commandline option to `--register-only`
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this is a dupe of #2446 , which has no description 

The intent of this ticket is that the certbot program would have a switch that will (only) create a new account (with TOS registration, etc).

There are at least 2 reasons for this:

1. a user may want to create an account / accept the TOS on "machine 1", but run certbot on "machine 2"
2. a user may want to use another client, but that client does not offer the account creation/management - and expects the account to be created on the official client. 

full disclosure: i wrote a client that I'm opensourcing and we're the second case.

issue_title: Why does `apt-get install` installs apache2?
issue_body:
On my server I had all my sites configured with nginx. After installing certbot via apt-get and doing some configurations for nginx, I restarted server and after that, all my sites were down.

This because, without my knowledge, an apache2 was up running, blocking the port and thus preventing nginx from starting up. Boom.

Is there no way to install certbot without apache2?

Ubuntu 14.04

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
```

apt-get install python-certbot-apache
```
according to https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/how-to-secure-apache-with-let-s-encrypt-on-ubuntu-16-04

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

issue_title: Don't mention locations of CSR and privkey
issue_body:
It seems like users are getting confused at least sometimes by Certbot's mention of how it's created a private key and CSR (*not* in the `live` directory!)

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/trouble-renewing-with-secondary-domains/32806

I would suggest that we change these messages so they at least don't print a full file path, since almost nobody will manage to use these files productively. I think the particular trouble is

https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/crypto_util.py#L63

and

https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/crypto_util.py#L98

issue_title: Use isatty checks before asking new questions
"Cert not yet due for renewal", but it expires on 16th January 2017 (It's 27th Dec. today)
issue_body:
Basically "certbot renew" should renew all certificates which expire within 30 days. My certificate does (my site is www.adnanber.be so you can check the certificate there), but certbot insists that it is not due for renewal (I even got an e-mail notification saying that it expires in 19 days).

The output when running certbot renew is:
```
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Processing /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/adnanber.be.conf
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Cert not yet due for renewal

The following certs are not due for renewal yet:
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/adnanber.be/fullchain.pem (skipped)
No renewals were attempted.
```

A little block from my nginx file (/etc/nginx/sites/adnanber.be)
```
    #ssl
    ssl on;
    ssl_certificate /etc/letsencrypt/live/adnanber.be/fullchain.pem;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/adnanber.be/privkey.pem;
```

My renewal file (/etc/letsencrypt/renewal/adnanber.be.conf)

```
# renew_before_expiry = 30 days
cert = /etc/letsencrypt/live/adnanber.be/cert.pem
privkey = /etc/letsencrypt/live/adnanber.be/privkey.pem
chain = /etc/letsencrypt/live/adnanber.be/chain.pem
fullchain = /etc/letsencrypt/live/adnanber.be/fullchain.pem
version = 0.9.3

# Options and defaults used in the renewal process
[renewalparams]
installer = None
authenticator = webroot
webroot_path = /etc/letsencrypt/live,
account = [REDACTED]
[[webroot_map]]
www.adnanber.be = /etc/letsencrypt/live
adnanber.be = /etc/letsencrypt/live
```

I'm not sure if the first row is supposed to be commented, or if there's supposed to be a comma at the end of the webroot_path row, but I tried removing them and got the same error, so I put them back like they used to be.

Anyway, certbot has worked fine for me for the past 2 or 3 times I used it to create and then renew. For some reason it just suddenly doesn't work now. I might just have done something retarded but I do need some help and guidance in how to solve this issue.

I've also restarted my nginx server and my remote machine, didn't help.

EDIT: Ok on my page it says 27th January, but the e-mail notification I got says 16th January. I'll wait until tomorrow and then run renew again.

There are some typos in the names of assigning_to_self_checker.py and invalid_range_index_checker.py  

    class AssigningToSelfChecker(BaseChecker):

    __implements__ = IAstroidChecker

    name = 'always_return'  

    class InvalidRangeIndexChecker(BaseChecker):

issue_title: Feature Request: enable simple frame compression without content-size header
issue_body:
The lz4 frame specification makes the content-size header field optional. I would love to be able to `lz4.frame.compress(foo, content_size=False)` and have the encoded frame not include the C.Size FLG and the optional 8 bytes of size data.

For example, the lz4f package does not include the content-size header and results in different bits:

```
In [1]: import lz4f
In [2]: lz4f.compressFrame(b'1234')
Out[2]: '\x04"M\x18`@\x82\x04\x00\x00\x801234\x00\x00\x00\x00'
In [3]: import lz4.frame
In [4]: lz4.frame.compress(b'1234')
Out[4]: '\x04"M\x18h@\x04\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\xcd\x04\x00\x00\x801234\x00\x00\x00\x00'
```

INTERNALERROR>   File "...../pytest_tap/plugin.py", line 62, in pytest_runtest_logreport
INTERNALERROR>     reason = report.longrepr[2].split(':', 1)[1].strip()
INTERNALERROR> AttributeError: ReprExceptionInfo instance has no attribute '__getitem__'

```
Offending code:
```
def pytest_runtest_logreport(report):
...
    elif report.outcome == 'skipped':
        reason = report.longrepr[2].split(':', 1)[1].strip()
```
Example of a test that crashes:
```
import pytest
@pytest.mark.xfail()
def test_fail():
    assert 1 == 2
```

issue_title: Query with Id IN array
issue_body:
Is it possible to query where Id is in an array of Ids? For example:

```
myArray = ["1234", "1233"]
sf.query("SELECT Id, Email FROM Contact WHERE Id IN :" + myArray)
```

issue_title: channel_manager.getChannelParticipants() returns an empty list
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

As can be seen from [this](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/888#issuecomment-322793739) comment the channel manager's [`getChannelParticipants()`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/smart_contracts/ChannelManagerContract.sol#L37) returns an empty list even if there are currently open channels.

We have experienced this in the testnet with a channel manager for the "Raiden Test Token".

It can be verified by the following for a token on ropsten:

```js
var channel_manager_address = "0xff88b0bc757631a3de768bcb89d2c0664fc8b88d";
var channel_manager_abi = [{"constant":true,"inputs":[],"name":"getChannelsParticipants","outputs":[{"name":"","type":"address[]"}],"payable":false,"type":"function"},{"constant":true,"inputs":[{"name":"partner","type":"address"}],"name":"getChannelWith","outputs":[{"name":"","type":"address"}],"payable":false,"type":"function"},{"constant":true,"inputs":[],"name":"getChannelsAddresses","outputs":[{"name":"","type":"address[]"}],"payable":false,"type":"function"},{"constant":true,"inputs":[{"name":"node_address","type":"address"}],"name":"nettingContractsByAddress","outputs":[{"name":"","type":"address[]"}],"payable":false,"type":"function"},{"constant":true,"inputs":[],"name":"tokenAddress","outputs":[{"name":"","type":"address"}],"payable":false,"type":"function"},{"constant":false,"inputs":[{"name":"partner","type":"address"},{"name":"settle_timeout","type":"uint256"}],"name":"newChannel","outputs":[{"name":"","type":"address"}],"payable":false,"type":"function"},{"inputs":[{"name":"token_address","type":"address"}],"payable":false,"type":"constructor"},{"payable":false,"type":"fallback"},{"anonymous":false,"inputs":[{"indexed":false,"name":"netting_channel","type":"address"},{"indexed":false,"name":"participant1","type":"address"},{"indexed":false,"name":"participant2","type":"address"},{"indexed":false,"name":"settle_timeout","type":"uint256"}],"name":"ChannelNew","type":"event"},{"anonymous":false,"inputs":[{"indexed":false,"name":"caller_address","type":"address"},{"indexed":false,"name":"partner","type":"address"}],"name":"ChannelDeleted","type":"event"}];
var channel_manager = web3.eth.contract(channel_manager_abi).at(channel_manager_address);
```

Figure out why. And if fixed then we can remove [this line](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/888/files#diff-b62876f9087286c4355ccbdf6dc7b2baR250) added by #888.

issue_title: Support `ipc` connection with ethereum node
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

At the moment we only support an `--rpc` connection with the underlying ethereum node.

## Solution

Make the necessary changes to also support the `--ipc` connection to the ethereum node.

issue_title: py3 compatibility
issue_body: Q: Shall we also break py2 compatibility and use new py3 features?

issue_title: Installation instructions are not complete
issue_body: I"m trying the installation on ubuntu 16.04. It looks like the Dockerfile has much more useful information than the README.md, which tells me to run python setup.py install. This fails immediately since I don't have setuptools installed :) But the real problem is the list of system packages which need to be installed in order to be able to compile the dependencies.

issue_title: Add zero errors when reading single file
issue_body:
In case user reads single file, then zero-filled column should be added to generated ASCII files.
If user says "--error none" then column with errors is not added.

issue_title: Number formatting in default converter
issue_body:
Default converter is saving numbers in Fortran style which is very inefficient for large files.

If we could break compatibility with old bdo2txt, then this style could be abandoned:
 - convertion could be faster
 - code will be cleaner

issue_title: Better identifier of temp scripts in `.sos`
issue_body:
Currently the exported temp scripts have file name `stepname_N1_N2`. However I've run into situations with multiple nested workflow, in the flavor of but much more complex than:  

```
[1]
sos_run('A:1+B:1')
[2]
sos_run('A:1+B:2')
...
```
what happen is that the file name `stepname_N1_N2` will get overwritten. It can be very annoying when errors occurred yet the pipeline did not stop immediately, and once it stopped it points to an updated version of `stepname_N1_N2` asking the user to run / debug, but in fact there is nothing wrong with that version of script. Can we use a better ID for these scripts that we can ensure that they are unique during the entire execution?

issue_title: Checking remote status before sending files.
issue_body:
Currently for each task, sos 

1. send files to remote host
2. send task to remote host
3. check remote status
4. execute task if needed.

We should perform 3 first because 1 and 2 can be slow if we do it each time, especially when we are resuming the execution of a workflow and basically only needs to retrieve results from remote host.

issue_title: Extract job ID from LSF bsub command.
issue_body:
Unlink qsub that returns a single job iD, `bsub` returns 

```
Job <607844> is submitted to queue <medium>
```

We should parse the output to get job ID and perhaps also queues.

issue_title: Improve error message for external tasks
issue_body:
I find the error prompt here is not ideal:

```
ERROR: 1a3273c5b127f0cb34aa5e2ac62f9d58 failed with RuntimeError error Failed to execute script (ret=1).
Please use command
	Rscript \
	  --default-packages=datasets,methods,utils,stats,grDevices,graphics \
	  /home/gaow/tmp/test/.sos/core_fl_0_16_e3e26db7.R
under "/home/gaow/tmp/test" to test it.
```

For this example the computation is quite heavy. It takes up to an hour to reproduce the error. When executed as non-task the error message would dump on the screen, which is ugly but can give people some hints. 

However here apparently one can see the error message via `sos status 1a3273c5b127f0cb34aa5e2ac62f9d58 -v 4`. That opens the err file which has more information including the prompt above. That can save people from re-running. I'm wondering if it is possible to distinguish between task/non-task case and ask users to use `sos status` to check the job, instead of suggesting a rerun.

issue_title: SoS performance dealing with large number of files
issue_body:
This bothers me when I do some very simple simulations:

```
[1]
output: [f"performance_test/{x+1}.out" for x in range(500)]
run:
  touch performance_test/{1..500}.out

[2]
r = [x for x in range(500)]
input: group_by = 1, paired_with = 'r'
output: [f"performance_test/{x+1}.rds" for x in range(500)], group_by = 1
task: concurrent = True
R: expand = '${ }'
  x = rnorm(${_r[0]})
  saveRDS(x, ${_output:r})
```

If you run this script, you'll see it halts for a second or 2 at the end of every batch of completed jobs. I can understand that things like signature checks etc are on going. Therefore a simple simulation that takes < 10 sec as a for loop can take as much as > 700 secs with SoS -- the overhead takes way longer time than the actual computation. I remember it used to be 10 sec vs > 100 sec before this summer. Now I guess as signature check becomes more strict and careful about racing conditions the whole process is a lot slower. Is there still room for optimization?

issue_title: Most pretrained networks have zero accuracy
issue_body:
The top-1 accuracy of most (but not all) pretrained models is close to zero,
```
Model            Top1 Acc
-------------  ----------
alexnet              0.52
densenet121          0
densenet161          0
densenet169          0
densenet201          0
resnet18             0
resnet34             0
resnet50             0
resnet101            0.02
resnet152            0
squeezenet1_0        0.62
squeezenet1_1        0.54
vgg11                0.64
vgg11_bn             0.06
vgg13                0.69
vgg13_bn             0.05
vgg16                0.69
vgg16_bn             0.02
vgg19                0.65
vgg19_bn             0.03
```
This was the output of the following test script (run on Python 3.5 with pytorch 0.2.0.1 and latest master of pytorch vision):
```
import torch
from torch.autograd import Variable
import torchvision.models as models
from PIL import Image
import numpy as np

models = [models.alexnet,
          models.densenet121,
          models.densenet161,
          models.densenet169,
          models.densenet201,
          models.resnet18,
          models.resnet34,
          models.resnet50,
          models.resnet101,
          models.resnet152,
          models.squeezenet1_0,
          models.squeezenet1_1,
          models.vgg11,
          models.vgg11_bn,
          models.vgg13,
          models.vgg13_bn,
          models.vgg16,
          models.vgg16_bn,
          models.vgg19,
          models.vgg19_bn]

def preprocess_fn(x):
    x = x / 255
    x -= np.array([0.485, 0.456, 0.406])[None,:,None,None]
    x /= np.array([0.229, 0.224, 0.225])[None,:,None,None]
    return x.copy()

import glob
images = glob.glob("images/imagenet/*.png")
data = []

for model in models:
    net = model(pretrained=True).cuda()

    top1 = 0

    for image_path in images[:100]:
        image = Image.open(image_path)
        image_size = 224
        image = image.resize((image_size, image_size), Image.ANTIALIAS)
        image = np.array(image).astype(np.float32)
        image = np.transpose(image, [2,0,1])
        label = int(image_path.split('.')[0].split('_')[1])

        image = torch.from_numpy(preprocess_fn(image[None])).cuda()
        image = Variable(image, volatile=True)
        q = net(image).data.cpu().numpy()[0]

        top1 += np.argmax(q) == label

    data.append([model.__name__, top1/100])
    print('{} reached Top-1 accuracy of {}.'.format(model.__name__, top1 / 100))
```

issue_title: zhmcclient.Client class has a misleading name
issue_body:
The `zhmcclient.Client` class is the target for operations such as `version_info()` and has the `cpcs` property. It would also be the class to be extended inthe future with new properties for HMC resources (such as users or groups).

That all suggests that the semantics of the class is really that it represents the HMC as a resource.

If we can agree so far, then two things should be done:

1. The description of the class should be updated to describe it as representing the HMC.
2. Optionally, we could also change its class name from `Client` to `Hmc` (keeping the current name as a deprecated alias name).

issue_title: 500 Error in LinkedIn Oauth2 Workflow
issue_body:
I'm trying to integrate python-social-auth into a Django site so users can login via LinkedIn.  I've followed the instructions (I hope correctly) and my app correctly sends the user off to LinkedIn to authorise the login.

Once authorised LinkedIn bounces the user back to `https://my-site/complete/linkedin-oauth2/?code=...`, and social-auth calls `https://api.linkedin.com/v1/people/~:(first-name,id,last-name)?format=json` to try and get some data for the user.

At this point LinkedIn returns a 500 error.  social-auth throws the exception `requests.exceptions.HTTPError: 500 Server Error: Internal Server Error for url: https://api.linkedin.com/v1/people/~:(first-name,id,last-name)?format=json`.

I've tried dialing up the logging on social/requests/httplib to DEBUG so all the headers are printed, and while I'm no oauth expert they look appropriate.  I wonder if this is a bug at LinkedIn's end but I'm hesitant to file a report until I know others are running into the same thing.

I'm running:
```$ pip freeze | grep social
python-social-auth==0.3.6
social-auth-app-django==2.1.0
social-auth-core==1.6.0```

issue_title: Remember resource object source locations
issue_body:
At the moment, in the translation from evaluation to a resource graph, we toss away some very valuable information about source locations.  This means that once we enter "resource realm", we lose our ability to deliver great diagnostics.  And, worst case, automatic edits will be impossible.

There is no technical reason for this limitation; we should preserve this information -- by tracking the "last writes" to resource properties -- and even serialize it so that it's entirely recoverable. 

As part of this, we may also want to consider the moral equivalent of a PDB file so that the source information can be stored optionally elsewhere, so as not to clutter up the checkpoints; it will almost certainly make them enormous, and diffs will be problematic.

issue_title: Workload hangs when plasma_manager is killed
issue_body:
<!--
General questions should be asked on the mailing list ray-dev@googlegroups.com.

Before submitting an issue, please fill out the following form.
-->

### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: Ubuntu 16.04
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: wheel from https://github.com/ray-project/ray/commit/f7c4f41df80b5b0b57068138e87c728ee609e392
- **Python version**: 3.6.3

### Describe the problem

Start a cluster on 10 m4.4xlarge as described here: http://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/using-ray-on-a-large-cluster.html

After the cluster is started, run this code:

```
@ray.remote
def f(x):
  i = 1
  return i

u = ray.put("hello")
%time sum(ray.get([f.remote(u) for i in range(100000)]))
```

While this is running, if we now kill a plasma_manager on one of the worker nodes, the workload hangs with the following message:

```
Remote function __main__.f failed with:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ubuntu/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/ray/worker.py", line 756, in _process_task
    args)
  File "/home/ubuntu/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/ray/worker.py", line 687, in _get_arguments_for_execution
    argument = self.get_object([arg])[0]
  File "/home/ubuntu/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/ray/worker.py", line 440, in get_object
    ray._config.worker_fetch_request_size())])
  File "pyarrow/plasma.pyx", line 552, in pyarrow.plasma.PlasmaClient.fetch (/ray/src/thirdparty/arrow/python/build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.6/plasma.cxx:6809)
  File "pyarrow/error.pxi", line 79, in pyarrow.lib.check_status (/ray/src/thirdparty/arrow/python/build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.6/lib.cxx:8192)
pyarrow.lib.ArrowIOError: Broken pipe


  You can inspect errors by running

      ray.error_info()

  If this driver is hanging, start a new one with

      ray.init(redis_address="34.234.100.132:6379")
```

issue_title: Never feel bad about taking time for yourself
issue_body:
Hello everyone,

This is primarily for maintainers but I wanted to make sure everyone was aware of this: https://nolanlawson.com/2017/03/05/what-it-feels-like-to-be-an-open-source-maintainer/

If for any reason you are feeling this way working on this project no one will blame you for taking a break, working on something else, or stepping back/working less. This is a big project with a lot of requests and issues and I want to make sure people don't get burned out. All the work by contributors, bug reporters, and maintainers is appreciated, but your mental health and general well being are more important than any project. 

This is probably not the right place to put this, but this is the easiest way to get the attention of everyone who regularly contributes. NEVER let this project negatively impact you, if it is I am behind you 100% doing what you need to do to maintain your own well being. Open source is tough, and taking care of yourself is the most critical aspect of any project.

issue_title: [i18n] Translation guidelines
issue_body:
As more people start translation Red. A issue has risen, the need for guidelines on translation. What do and don't we translate and how do we go with setting those. 

### Server or Guild
Right now the source files both have server and guild. Its better to stick with one for consistency. Which one should we pick? I personally think server since its seen on the Discord UI this way.

### Translate core keywords
Core keywords (the ones described in the glossary, yes we have a glossary) are keywords such as ``server``, ``cog`` and so on. Should we translate these?
If we don't translate these, this could be confusing for the end user. However if we do translate them, then it could cause a problem for supporting a user.

issue_title: Make output widgets specific to widget manager with private rendermime reference
issue_body:
Output widgets depend very much on the widget manager. Right now, this forces the widget manager to provide interfaces for things like the document rendermime and the kernel context - things we generally don't want widgets to rely on because they are very specific to the widget manager.

It may be cleaner to special-case the output widget, so that it has a private reference to the rendermime and kernel context, but not expose those things to general widgets.

issue_title: Dashboarding discussion
issue_body:
To keep this archived somewhere, here are the notes from our discussion on dashboarding at the ipywidgets workshop in Paris in Jan 2018 (see https://github.com/OpenDreamKit/OpenDreamKit/issues/246, and https://www.lri.fr/etherpad/p/jupyter-widgets for the notes).

We can get an overview of current solutions to displaying the widgets on a web page, as sort of a web app. For example, Pascal's ipywidget-server, https://github.com/oschuett/appmode, or https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/pull/1862

Pascal's solution: a separate server that is instantiated with the code to be run, a specific kernel https://github.com/pbugnion/ipywidgets_server

https://github.com/oschuett/appmode - strip out the classic notebook UI

https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/pull/1862 (not finished) - read a notebook, execute all cells from the browser, display specific cell outputs

IBM Jupyter Dashboard server - easy drag-and-drop layout of cell outputs in a grid, one-click publish, secure execution of the notebook by not accepting code execution from the browser
https://github.com/jupyter-attic/dashboards_server and https://github.com/jupyter/dashboards  and https://github.com/jupyter-attic/dashboards_bundlers

Do we reuse kernels (e.g., you give a link to someone with a widget from a specific running kernel), or start a new kernel for *every* dashboard view

    - Different kinds of uses cases: dashboards as "isolated" kernel execution (each dashboard has own kernel) vs dashboard as "broadcast" enviornment - by interacting with one I am affecting all others.

Can we expose a layout widget that lets you drag and drop to layout things, and then save the layout.

Integration with kernel gateway / remote kernel manager?

It's not obvious we need a live separate kernel. For some usecases, you may want to execute the code in-process (e.g., flask, django).

Should the layout depend on widgets and the kernel? Or should the layout be independent of widgets/kernel.
  - Making two layout systems can be confusing (a frontend UI layout, and a widget layout)
  - We could make a layout widget that lets you drag and drop children to lay out them. Then save the state of the widget.

Do we force the authoring environment to be a notebook? An authoring environment like shiny? 

Perhaps we just have some very specific predefined layouts that can just be populated with children.

If we were able to publish a binder-ready repo for a dashboard, that could take care of a deployment usecase.

Needs:
    - Subject-matter experts (know python, not js) easily expose their code/explorations as a webapp to stakeholders with no programming skills, so they don't want any notion of a notebook, code, etc. Code might be running internal software stacks, etc., so we don't have to distribute software, just expose a GUI to use the tools.
    - Students exploring learning materials (similar to above usecase)
    - Should be usable from different language kernels, such as R
    - Layout should be doable programmatically
    - E-posters at an academic conference (three-column layout)
    - Include markdown/text/html, possibly with variable substitutions
    - Security - not being able to execute arbitrary code from the browser - needs to be an option
    - Programmatic layouts should use standard layout protocols, e.g., flexbox, grid, etc.
    - Other styling, such as colors, fonts, etc.
    -

issue_title: Should SendStream automatically serialize calls to send_all?
issue_body:
(Context: discussion with @agronholm at https://gitter.im/python-trio/general?at=59c03f58c101bc4e3ae28c81)

Currently, if two tasks call `SendStream.send_all` at the same time, then the second one raises an error, and if you want to write to the same stream from multiple tasks then you have to protect the stream with a `Lock`. We could alternatively require that stream implementations make this "just work", basically moving the lock inside `send_all`.

Changing this wouldn't be a compat breaker for the users of the `Stream` API, because it would convert an error into something that works. But it would be a compat breaker for implementors of the `Stream` API, because now their users might start depending on this new behavior. So we should probably make a decision sooner rather than later.

# receive_some

For `receive_some`, I don't think this makes sense; if you have multiple tasks trying to read from the same stream then you generally need to rethink things. (How do you make sure that the correct bytes go to the correct task? There's no easy answer.) Of course, it's also hard to make `receive_some` actually broken even if we did allow concurrent calls – there's no equivalent to "interleaving" like can happen with `send_all`. But it will raise errors sometimes if there's no explicit locking, because `trio.hazmat.wait_readable` raises errors if two tasks try to block in it at the same time. ...I guess really this is exactly the same cases where it raises an error now with the explicit conflict detection though, give or take a `checkpoint`.

# send_all - is it possible?

For one task calling `send_all` and another calling `wait_send_all_might_not_block`, or two tasks calling `wait_send_all_might_not_block`... bleh. Giving an error is fairly reasonable, but maybe we can do better. If we allow two tasks to concurrently do:
```python
while True:
    await stream.send_all(...)
```
then we should probably also allow two tasks to concurrently do:
```python
while True:
    await stream.wait_send_all_might_not_block()
    await stream.send_all(...)
```
which would mean supporting that all combinations of `send_all` and `wait_send_all_might_not_block`.

What if we simply protected both methods with the same lock?

- `wait_send_all_might_not_block` is holding the lock, `send_all` arrives: `send_all` ends up blocking until `wait_send_all_might_not_block` returns. Ok, sure, by definition this was going to happen anyway. The `send_all` might mean that whoever called `wait_send_all_might_not_block` is surprised because it does block, but that's part of the contract anyway (hence the word `might`)

- `send_all` is holding the lock, `wait_send_all_might_not_block` arrives: an immediate call to `send_all` would block until it got the lock, so `wait_send_all_might_not_block` *should* block until the lock is available. OK.

- `wait_send_all_might_not_block` is holding the lock, `wait_send_all_might_not_block` arrives: this is a little weird, but I guess it works out ok. The second one can't proceed until the first one returns. But we know that the first one will return as soon as `send_all` might not block, so this can't directly cause the second one to block longer than it should have. And then when it finally gets the lock, it should detect the same stream state that the first one did, and return immediately. (Unless something else happened in the mean time to change the state, but in that case blocking longer is appropriate anyway.)

# is it a good idea?

So, it looks like we could potentially change this. Is it a good idea?

In theory a `trio.Lock` is a little more heavyweight than a `trio._util.ConflictDetector`, but the difference is pretty minor. Mostly the `Lock` needs a bit more memory to hold the `ParkingLot` used when there's contention; a `ParkingLot` is just an object holding an `OrderedDict`. `OrderedDict` is bigger than you'd think (`sys.getsizeof(OrderedDict()) == 416` on my laptop), but we could allocate it lazily if it really became an issue, and `Stream`s are somewhat substantial objects anyway (they generally hold kernel buffers, etc.). And acquiring/releasing an uncontended `Lock` is barely any more expensive than acquiring/releasing a `ConflictDetector`. One difference is that blocking to get a `Lock` requires async context, but for `send_all` and friends this is fine.

A bigger question is whether this actually gives more friendly semantics. It's certainly convenient for message-based protocols like websocket, where you might want to have a `WSConnection` object where you do `await conn.send_message("...")` which packs that string into a websocket frame and passes the frame into the underlying byte-stream in a single call -- right now this requires explicit locking if you want `send_message` to be usable from different tasks, and if `send_all` did its own locking then *potentially* it wouldn't. Specifically, it would work OK if you make sure to write `send_message` so that all the internal protocol state manipulation happens synchronously, and then the `send_all` call happens at the end. This is probably the most natural way to write this (especially if working on top of a sans-io library like wsproto), but it's certainly possible to get it wrong if you don't pay attention. OTOH if streams require users to do their own locking, then the natural way to do this locking is to put it around the *whole* `send_message` body, and then you don't have to worry about auditing `send_message` to make sure that it contains exactly one checkpoint.

There are also ways of working with streams that are inherently task-unsafe regardless of what kind of implicit locking we do. E.g. still with the websocket example, someone could write:
```python
async def send_message(self, body):
    await self.transport.send_all(self._make_frame_header(body))
    await self.transport.send_all(self._make_frame_body(body))
```
Now this method is definitely not safe to call concurrently from multiple tasks. If you do it anyway, then with the current design, it may or may not raise an error and point out the problem; with implicit locking, it definitely never raises an error. So arguably the current design does better here?

Someone recently found some bugs in some subtle `contextlib.contextmanager` corner cases. The fixes are here: https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/949/files ([discussion](https://bugs.python.org/issue29692))

issue_title: Support for TCP fast open / TLS early data
issue_body:
TCP has a feature now where you can send some data along with the initial SYN packet, called TCP fast open ([LWN article](https://lwn.net/Articles/508865/), [rfc](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7413)). (At least, if you've made a connection to the same server somewhat recently.) Linux has actually supported this for quite some time (some support since 3.7, complete support since 3.16). This is a bit tricky because the point of the 3-way handshake is to establish a reliable connection, and this lets you send data before that, so it's intrinsically unreliable. In particular a server might randomly seem to get multiple connections and read this data multiple times, even though the client only made one connection, so you can only use this as an opt-in thing with protocols where you know the initial data being sent is idempotent.

TLS 1.3 has a semantically very similar feature, where you can send data along with the initial handshake, called "early data". It has the same problem with requiring idempotency (and the same requirement that you have made a connection to the server before), so it has to be opt-in. It doesn't actually exist as something one can use yet though. (Needs openssl 1.1.1, plus support in wrappers.)

We should support these in our high-level API on both the client and server side. I think that on the client side, this would mean something like an extra `early_data=` argument to `open_tcp_stream` or `open_ssl_over_tcp_stream`. And on the server side, something like an `allow_early_data` argument to `open_tcp_listeners`/`open_ssl_over_tcp_listeners` (defaulting to `False`)? I guess for `open_tcp_listeners` this should be a "queue length" (to avoid DoS issues).

For TLS early open, we'll also want to expose some of the richer API that [openssl exposes](https://github.com/openssl/openssl/blob/master/doc/man3/SSL_read_early_data.pod) on the `SSLStream` API. (Or maybe this is the only thing we want to do on the server side?) For TCP fast open, AFAICT the socket API doesn't give any way to distinguish between regular data and fast-open data, so this is unnecessary.

Also, I believe that in general the initial part of a TLS client handshake is always idempotent, so `open_ssl_over_tcp_stream` should use TCP fast open by default, and likewise for `open_ssl_over_tcp_listeners`.

However, there is a subtlety when TLS early data is combined with TCP fast open: there is a rule that servers are supposed to reject re-use of TLS early data tokens, so (a) on the server side maybe we need to help implement that somehow?, (b) on the client side, we can't necessarily make one `SSLObject` and then pass its data to `open_tcp_stream`, because if happy-eyeballs kicks in then we'll end up sending that same data multiple times. OTOH maybe this is fine because (a) happy eyeballs rarely kicks in, and (b) when it does whichever connection wins is usually going to be the one whose SYN was received first, and (c) even if we do end up using a connection where the token was invalidated by another happy-eyeballs connection, TLS transparently falls back on a regular handshake. I think.

Testing this is going to be super annoying. I'm not sure how to even do it except by like, shelling out to `tcpdump` or something.

Cross-platform support will also be a bit tricky. On Windows you have to set [`TCP_FASTOPEN`](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms738596(v=vs.85).aspx) and then use [`ConnectEx`](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms737606(v=vs.85).aspx), which means you MUST use IOCP. On MacOS, you have to use the odd `connectx`, which Python doesn't currently wrap.

This page has some useful notes: https://dnsprivacy.org/wiki/display/DP/TCP+Fast+Open#TCPFastOpen-OSX

issue_title: Version required is wrong, 3.5 features in use in JSONModelRelation
issue_body:
Currently setup.py claims that we're useavle on 3.4+, but I used dict splatting in literals, which hit in 3.5, and didn't catch it bc I use 3.6 to develop.

We should either rewrite that line to use dict.update, or specify 3.5 as min version; given the age of 3.4, I'd be inclined to latter, but I know there's a lot of old Python installs, and Red Hat's antique sensibilities, etc.

issue_title: Staging failure should result in a retry
issue_body: If retries are enabled, staging failure should result in a retry.

issue_title: Add utils to automatically detect monitored resources.
issue_body:
Add utils to automatically detect monitored resources based the environment that the application is running in, according to [OpenCensus MonitoredResource specs](https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-specs/blob/master/utils/MonitoredResource.md). This util should be independent of specific exporters, since it's supposed to be share between Stackdriver Stats exporter and Stackdriver Trace exporter.

Counterpart in Java: https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-java/tree/master/contrib/monitored_resource_util.

issue_title: Layered templates with the same name
issue_body:
I must admit I am not sure this is a great idea...but IMO is worth discussing.

Currently it is not possible to have two templates with the same name.  That prevents a local one vs a global one.

One example:

1. local `.moban.dt/MANIFEST.in.jj2` with block 'more_options'
2. https://github.com/moremoban/pypi-mobans/blob/master/templates/MANIFEST.in.jj2

Another:

1. local `.moban.dt/setup.py.jj2` with blocks
2. https://github.com/moremoban/pypi-mobans/blob/master/templates/setup.py.jj2

That could be confusing, but then creating local templates with variant template names is also confusing, and annoying as they will have a common and unnecessary prefix.

issue_title: Memory usage does not work with class methods?
issue_body:
I want to profile time and memory usage of class method. 
When I try to use `partial` from `functools` I got this error:

```
File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/memory_profiler.py", line 126, in memory_usage
  aspec = inspect.getargspec(f)
File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/inspect.py", line 815, in getargspec
  raise TypeError('{!r} is not a Python function'.format(func))
TypeError: <functools.partial object at 0x252da48> is not a Python function
```

By the way exactly the same approach works fine with `timeit` function.

When I try to use `lambda` as was I got this error:

```
File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/memory_profiler.py", line 141, in memory_usage
  ret = parent_conn.recv()
IOError: [Errno 4] Interrupted system call
```

How can I handle class methods with memory_profiler? Are there any (even dirty) ways?

I asked this question on SO: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/16593246/how-to-use-memory-profiler-python-module-with-class-methods

issue_title: DMARC record copy at root level results in no DMARC record at all
issue_body:
A domain like danfoss.com has a copy of the DMARC record placed at the root of the domain.
This results in checkdmarc returning:
  "dmarc": {
    "record": null,
    "valid": false,
    "location": null,
    "error": "The DMARC record must be located at _dmarc.danfoss.com, not danfoss.com"
  }
While it's wrong to have a DMARC record at root level, it should not result in the real DMARC record being discarded

issue_title: Fix environment variable encodings in `vistir.misc.run`
[BUG] UnicodeEncodeError in Idle with fix
issue_body:
###Note
Sorry for the cross post, I'm still new to git:) I've implemented the suggestion below in PU #153.

### Command Ran
```
import twint

c = twint.Config()
c.Store_csv = True
c.Output = '../path/to/sheet.csv'
c.Since = '2017-06-16'
c.Until = '2017-06-25'
c.Search = """canneslions OR cannes OR platformforaction OR
                OR googlebeach OR cannes2017 OR canneslions2017 OR
               agencylife OR google"""
c.Lang ='en'
c.Format = '''id: {id} | date: {date} | time: {time} | user_id: {user_id} | username: {username} |
              tweet: {tweet} | timezone: {timezone} | hashtags: {hashtags} 
              replies: {replies} | retweets: {retweets} | likes: {likes}'''

twint.run.Search(c)
```


### Description of Issue
The above code produces a UnicodeEncodeError relating to printing in Idle, see [this](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/32442608/ucs-2-codec-cant-encode-characters-in-position-1050-1050). Traceback below:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/path/to/my/script.py", line 21, in <module>
    twint.run.Search(c)
  File "/anaconda3/envs/twitter_scrape/lib/python3.6/site-packages/twint/run.py", line 25, in Search
    run(search.Search(config).main())
  File "/anaconda3/envs/twitter_scrape/lib/python3.6/site-packages/twint/run.py", line 5, in run
    get_event_loop().run_until_complete(x)
  File "/anaconda3/envs/twitter_scrape/lib/python3.6/asyncio/base_events.py", line 468, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "/anaconda3/envs/twitter_scrape/lib/python3.6/site-packages/twint/search.py", line 49, in main
    await self.tweets()
  File "/anaconda3/envs/twitter_scrape/lib/python3.6/site-packages/twint/search.py", line 37, in tweets
    await output.Tweets(tweet, "", self.config, self.conn)
  File "/anaconda3/envs/twitter_scrape/lib/python3.6/site-packages/twint/output.py", line 59, in Tweets
    _output(tweet, output, config)
  File "/anaconda3/envs/twitter_scrape/lib/python3.6/site-packages/twint/output.py", line 45, in _output
    print(output)
UnicodeEncodeError: 'UCS-2' codec can't encode characters in position 389-389: Non-BMP character not supported in Tk
```

### Environment Details
Using OSX 10.13.3, twint 1.1.3.4, running using Idle with python 3.6.5


### Fix
To fix this, I changed line 45 in `output.py` from:

```
print(output)
```

to:

```
try:
    print(output)
except UnicodeEncodeError:
    pass
```

issue_title: Problems creating virtualenv on Windows when Python is not installed for all users.
issue_body:
I'm not a Windows user, so I could be missing something, but this is what I'm seeing (ran into it trying to get the pip test suite running on Windows):

S:>virtualenv --no-site-packages blah
New python executable in blah\Scripts\python.exe
ERROR: The executable blah\Scripts\python.exe is not functioning
ERROR: It thinks sys.prefix is 'c:\python25' (should be 's:\blah')
ERROR: virtualenv is not compatible with this system or executable

S:>c:

C:>virtualenv --no-site-packages yo
New python executable in yo\Scripts\python.exe
Installing setuptools..............done.

Stefano Masini wrote on 2009-12-03:

I had the same problem.
I fixed it by reinstalling Python2.6. The difference is that the first time (when virtualenv didn't work) I had installed it with the option "Only for this user", while the next time (when it worked) I reinstalled Python with the option "For all users".

Ian Bicking wrote on 2009-12-03:

I've put in an extra error message on Windows when this occurs, suggesting that the user install Python for all users, and pointing them at this bug.

Robert Myers wrote on 2010-03-15:

The problem is that the pythonXX.dll is missing when you install for 'only this user'. Copying the correct dll into the virtual env scripts folder fixes this issue.

Anywhere before the test "[py_executable, '-c', 'import sys; print sys.prefix']" place the lines:

    # Fix python on windows if installed for 'only this user'
    if sys.platform == 'win32':
        # Windows 'may' be installed for 'Only this user' so lets fix it and try again.
        python_dll = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(sys.executable),
            'python%s%s.dll' % (sys.version_info[0], sys.version_info[1]))
        if os.path.isfile(python_dll):
            shutil.copyfile(python_dll, os.path.join(os.path.dirname(py_executable),
                'python%s%s.dll' % (sys.version_info[0], sys.version_info[1])))

Torsten Knodt wrote on 2010-07-27:

I have a Python 2.5.1 installation made by a Windows software into C:\Program Files\Common Files...\Python25. The python25.dll is in C:\WINNT\SYSTEM32 so I guess it's a installation for "All users" but virtualenv bails out with the error messages described in this bug.

Any suggestions?
